title: Peppermint bathroom freshener powder
subtitle: A dry bicarbonate and citric acid deodoriser powder with peppermint for the toilet bowl
excerpt: Bicarbonate of soda neutralises acid-based bathroom odours; citric acid neutralises alkaline odours; peppermint essential oil adds a clean, sharp scent. Dropped into the toilet bowl, the powder fizzes as the two react in water, agitating the bowl and releasing the peppermint fragrance. Store as a loose powder and scoop as needed. Yield is 200 g.
sourceNotes: Bicarbonate of soda as an odour neutraliser: neutralises acidic odour compounds such as butyric acid (the smell of vomit) and other volatile organic acids. Citric acid neutralises alkaline odours and reacts with bicarbonate in water to fizz. The fizzing is the same chemistry as bath bombs: CO2 released in an acid-base reaction. Peppermint EO: the phenolic menthol compounds give the characteristic sharp, cooling scent. Dry storage: the two ingredients will not react in dry conditions; they react when they contact water in the bowl.

recipe.yieldDescription: 200 g powder (approximately 10 doses of 20 g each)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale by multiplying all weights. Store in an airtight container.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use immediately. Keep sealed; moisture activates the reaction.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 6 months in an airtight container in a dry place. Bathroom humidity gradually activates the acid-base reaction; the powder will lose some fizz over time. Discard if the powder has clumped into a solid mass.

glossaryTerms.definition: The elimination of odour-causing compounds by chemical reaction rather than by masking with fragrance. Bicarbonate of soda neutralises acidic odour compounds; citric acid neutralises alkaline compounds. Together they cover both sides of the odour-causing spectrum.

content.text: Bicarbonate of soda and citric acid both give

: bicarbonate tackles acid smells; citric acid tackles the rest. When the powder hits water, acid and base react and fizz. Peppermint oil mixes into the dry powder and releases its clean scent when the powder hits water. Keep completely dry; even bathroom steam can slowly trigger the reaction, so use an airtight container.

items.prepNote: Fine grade; neutralises acidic odour compounds
Dry form; neutralises alkaline odour compounds and triggers fizzing with the bicarbonate
Add last and stir in briskly so the oil absorbs into the dry powder

content.text: Weigh the bicarbonate of soda and citric acid into a dry bowl. Stir to combine. Add the peppermint essential oil and stir briskly; the oil will absorb into the dry powder within a minute. Transfer immediately to an airtight jar or tin and seal. Keep completely dry between uses.

Scoop approximately 2 tablespoons (about 20 g) into the toilet bowl. The powder will fizz on contact with the water and release the peppermint scent. Leave for 5 minutes, then brush and flush. For deodorising the bathroom air, a small saucer of plain bicarbonate of soda placed in the corner absorbs ambient odours; this powder is specifically for the bowl.

Store in an airtight container in a dry location, not inside a damp bathroom cabinet if possible. Keeps 6 months. If the powder has partially clumped but not hardened solid, it will still fizz; break up any lumps before use. Discard if the powder has hardened completely into a single mass.
